skill.name: virtual-machine-management
skill.description: Manage Proxmox virtual machines, including lifecycle actions, configuration review, cloning, templates, and resource planning.
skill.summary: Manage Proxmox VM lifecycle, configuration, templates, cloning, and resource optimization.
skill.author: DataKnifeAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and network reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are false positives in Markdown prose and inline tool names. No executable code, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration was found in SKILL.md. The skill is still medium risk because it guides high-impact Proxmox VM operations, including stop, reboot, configuration changes, cloning, templating, and deletion.

medium_findings.title: High-Impact VM Lifecycle Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill describes administrative tools that can start, stop, reboot, suspend, resume, clone, update, and delete Proxmox virtual machines. These actions are legitimate for this skill, but misuse could cause downtime, resource exhaustion, or data loss, so operator confirmation and access controls are important.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool list and examples explicitly include destructive or availability-impacting VM operations. The intent is legitimate administration, so this is operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution, but the lines contain inline tool names and example tool invocations. No executable Ruby, shell script, subprocess call, or command interpolation is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible in Markdown documentation around tool names and examples. There is no code file or runtime execution context in the scanned skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Detections Are Prose False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings occur on ordinary description and VM planning lines. No hash function, cipher, encryption routine, key generation, or cryptographic API usage appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are natural-language descriptions about Proxmox VMs and node placement. They contain no cryptographic operation or weak algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Static Detection Is a Prose False Positive
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance finding points to a best-practice sentence about graceful VM shutdown. No port scanning, host discovery, packet probing, or network enumeration instruction appears at that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line discusses minimizing data loss during shutdown. It does not reference network scanning tools or reconnaissance behavior.

content.value_statement: Proxmox VM operations can be slow and error-prone when teams must inspect many nodes and configurations manually. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide VM lifecycle, configuration, template, clone, and optimization workflows.
actual_capabilities: Lists virtual machines on a specified Proxmox node.
Retrieves VM status, configuration, and performance details.
Guides VM lifecycle actions such as start, shutdown, reboot, suspend, and resume.
Supports VM creation, advanced configuration, cloning, and template-based deployment workflows.
Explains resource allocation checks and optimization planning for VM placement.
Provides operational response formats for VM listings, status confirmations, and utilization analysis.
limitations: Requires the underlying Proxmox tools or MCP server to be configured and authorized.
Does not replace Proxmox role-based access control or change approval processes.
Destructive operations can cause data loss if used without backups and confirmation.
Does not include its own monitoring agent, scheduler, or rollback implementation.

use_cases.title: Daily VM Operations
use_cases.description: Check VM status, review resource usage, and perform controlled lifecycle actions during routine operations.

use_cases.title: Template-Based Provisioning
use_cases.description: Create a prepared VM, mark it as a template, and clone new instances for repeatable deployments.

use_cases.title: Capacity and Placement Review
use_cases.description: Review VM configurations and plan resource distribution across nodes before changes or migrations.

prompt_templates.title: List VMs on a Node
prompt_templates.prompt: List all virtual machines on the selected Proxmox node and summarize their status, CPU, memory, and disk allocation.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect One VM
prompt_templates.prompt: Check VM 100 on node pve2. Show its current status, configuration highlights, and any resource concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Create From a Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Clone template VM 100 into a new full VM named web-server-01. Confirm the planned VM ID, node, and resource settings before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize VM Placement
prompt_templates.prompt: Review VM distribution across the available Proxmox nodes and recommend placement changes that reduce resource contention and downtime risk.

output_examples.input: List all VMs on the production node.
output_examples.output: A concise table-style summary of VM IDs, names, power states, CPU allocation, memory allocation, disk usage, and notable alerts.

output_examples.input: Clone the template VM for a new web server.
output_examples.output: A confirmation plan that names the source template, target VM ID, target node, new VM name, clone mode, and post-clone verification steps.
A troubleshooting summary covering VM status, configured resources, recent lifecycle state, performance indicators, and recommended next checks.

best_practices: Confirm node, VM ID, and intended action before any lifecycle or deletion operation.
Use graceful shutdown and recent backups before disruptive maintenance or configuration changes.
Keep templates documented, patched, and tested before cloning production workloads.
anti_patterns: Deleting or stopping VMs without confirming owner, workload, and backup status.
Cloning outdated templates that contain stale packages, credentials, or configuration drift.
Increasing CPU, memory, or disk allocation without checking node capacity first.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Proxmox virtual machines?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents basic and advanced VM creation workflows when the required Proxmox tools are available.

faq.question: Can it delete a VM?
faq.answer: Yes. Deletion is documented, but it should require explicit confirmation because it can remove data.

faq.question: Does it manage VM templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to mark a prepared VM as a template and clone new VMs from it.

faq.question: Does it include direct Proxmox credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The skill file is documentation and does not include credentials or secret access.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The file has no malicious code, but it guides high-impact infrastructure actions that can affect availability and data.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
